text.title: Procurement Management Framework Failure Modes
text.summary: 4 antipatterns observed in real deployments. Each pairs symptom + root cause + concrete fix.

antipattern.symptom: Selection driven by lowest unit price with no quality or risk weighting.
antipattern.root-cause: Price-only ignores ramp-up cost, quality gap, and management overhead; TCO usually exceeds a higher-priced reliable vendor.
antipattern.fix: Apply pre-published weighted rubric; price weight ≤30% unless commoditised.

antipattern.symptom: Reference checks summarised from the vendor's own proposal.
antipattern.root-cause: Vendor-controlled references are marketing; independent voices are required.
antipattern.fix: Call previous clients directly; ask about scope creep, change behaviour, exit handover.

antipattern.symptom: SOW accepted with un-numbered deliverables and prose-only acceptance language.
antipattern.root-cause: Every undefined deliverable becomes a dispute; a clear SOW saves months of rework.
antipattern.fix: Enforce SOW template: deliverable IDs, AC table, verification method per AC.

antipattern.symptom: Contract executed with no change-control clause.
antipattern.root-cause: Scope evolves; without a written change process, additions are either rejected or silently absorbed.
antipattern.fix: Require change-process clause: written request → impact analysis → signed addendum.
